elements.sent-2411-001: Autonomous driving requires a comprehensive understanding of 3D environments to facilitate high-level tasks such as motion prediction, planning, and mapping.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper, we introduce DriveMLLM, a benchmark specifically designed to evaluate the spatial understanding capabilities of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) in autonomous driving.
elements.sent-2411-003: DriveMLLM includes 2,734 front-facing camera images and introduces both absolute and relative spatial reasoning tasks, accompanied by linguistically diverse natural language questions.
elements.sent-2411-004: To measure MLLMs' performance, we propose novel evaluation metrics focusing on spatial understanding.
elements.sent-2411-005: We evaluate several state-of-the-art MLLMs on DriveMLLM, and our results reveal the limitations of current models in understanding complex spatial relationships in driving contexts.
elements.sent-2411-006: We believe these findings underscore the need for more advanced MLLM-based spatial reasoning methods and highlight the potential for DriveMLLM to drive further research in autonomous driving.
elements.sent-2411-008: One of the primary tasks for vision-based autonomous driving system is to achieve holistic 3D scene understanding from visual images or videos~\cite{barabas2017current , which essentially serve fundamental information for further high-level tasks such as motion prediction~\cite{vectornet,tnt,liu2021multimodal,gu2021densetnt,nayakanti2023wayformer , planning~\cite{pomerleau1988alvinn,bansal2018chauffeurnet,li2023uncertainty and map construction~\cite{xiong2023neuralmapprior,vision_semanmap .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: While we have witnessed huge progress in per-object centered recognition tasks with the assistance of various large scale dataset~\cite{Cordts2016Cityscapes, geiger2013vision, RobotCarDatasetIJRR , including detection~\cite{arrow_roadmarking,realtime_vehicle_det , tracking~\cite{SoDA,od_track_ijrr , optical flow estimation~\cite{dong2024memflow,luo2024flowdiffuser and semantic segmentation~\cite{Chen2017ROADRO,Hetang_2024_CVPR , the inter-object spatial relation reasoning from RGB images in autonomous driving has been ignored even though its vital importance in achieving fully holistic 3D scene understanding.}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-010: In computer vision, within image spatial relation reasoning has been thoroughly studied~\cite{sparel_iceei with the assistance of large-scale visual genome dataset~\cite{visual_genome .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: However, prior works mainly focus on simple relations.
elements.sent-2411-012: For example, the left/right and top/bottom are relative position relations.
elements.sent-2411-013: These simple relations are far from fully representing the potential complex spatial relations that are present in the 3D scene.
elements.sent-2411-014: The emergence of large language models~(LLM)~\cite{gpt4, team2023gemini in recent years has largely unlocked the potential to address various high-level vision tasks~\cite{ chen2023palix, liu2024visual, liu2024llavanext, alayrac2022flamingo, bai2023qwenvl, wang2023cogvlm, dai2023instructblip, chen2023sharegpt4v, internlmxcomposer2, lu2023unified .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Although the aforementioned huge advancement and dramatic progress we have achieved in various multimodal large language models~(MLLM) based tasks, the multimodal large language models based spatial understanding in autonomous driving still awaits to be tackled.
elements.sent-2411-016: As discussed in the last two paragraphs, pre-LLM era spatial understanding unanimously focuses on per-object recognition tasks.
elements.sent-2411-017: However, inter-object spatial understanding is essential to achieve high-level and holistic autonomous driving scene understanding.
elements.sent-2411-018: Benefiting from the huge potential and powerfulness of LLM and the progress we have witnessed in recent years, we propose to tackle high-level, inter-object spatial understanding by leveraging MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-019: In this paper, we propose a large-scale benchmark, dubbed DriveMLLM, tailored for autonomous driving spatial understanding tasks with MLLMs.
elements.sent-2411-020: We meticulously curate DriveLLM from the nuScenes~\cite{nuScenes dataset, a large-scale autonomous driving dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-021: As the nuScenes~\cite{nuScenes dataset contains images from six cameras covering a full $360^\circ$ field of view, it serves as an ideal data source for constructing DriveMLLM benchmark and ensures the curated benchmark is grounded in realistic driving scenarios.}
elements.sent-2411-022: Specifically, we focused on the images from the front-facing camera in the validation split of the nuScenes~\cite{nuScenes dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-023: We have curated 880 images with objects of interest that have a clear appearance.
elements.sent-2411-024: For the spatial relations, we focus on both absolute spatial reasoning and relative positional relationship reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-025: We further construct linguistically diverse and contextually similar natural language questions (as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:intro ) and propose new corresponding evaluation metrics to evaluate the spatial understanding capabilities of various MLLMs.}
elements.sent-2411-026: We comprehensively evaluate DriveMLLM on various MLLM models, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:radar .}
elements.sent-2411-027: In summary, we make three main contributions to this work:.
elements.sent-2411-028: We propose a large benchmark, called DriveMLLM, for spatial understanding in autonomous driving with multimodal large language models~(MLLMs), a research topic that has not yet been discussed of vital importance.
elements.sent-2411-029: We base DriveMLLM on nuScenes~\cite{nuScenes dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-030: It comprises 880 images and diverse natural language-based questions.
elements.sent-2411-031: DriveMLLM introduces both absolute and relative spatial relation reasoning tasks, which is essential for achieving holistic autonomous driving scene understanding.
elements.sent-2411-032: We verify DriveMLLM on various MLLM models, showing the incapability of most MLLM models in autonomous driving spatial understanding.
elements.sent-2411-033: In turn, it exhibits the importance and huge potential of our introduced DriveMLLM benchmark for facilitating further research.

elements.sent-2411-034: Benefiting from the huge success in large language models~(LLMs)~\cite{gpt3,gpt4,gpt4o in recent years, a new research venue has been focusing on extending natural language-based large models~(especially the GPT family LLM) to multimodal large language models~(MLLM)~\cite{alayrac2022flamingo,li2023blip,li2023blip2,radford2021learning,sun2023eva,fang2023eva .}}
elements.sent-2411-035: Among all of them, encompassing vision into language has made dramatic progress and various vision language models~(VLM) have been developed~\cite{qwenvl, Qwen-VL, li2023blip, li2023blip2, liu2023improvedllava, liu2024llavanext for various crossmodal tasks such as visual question answer~(VQA)~\cite{antol2015vqa,balanced_vqa_v2 and crossmodal reasoning~\cite{zellers2019vcr,hu2022promptcap,fu-etal-2022-theres,schwenk2022okvqa , owing to the availability of various large image-text datasets~\cite{lin2014microsoft,krishna2017visual,schuhmann2021laion,changpinyo2021cc12m .}}}}

elements.sent-2411-041: In the pre-LLM era, most public multimodal vision-language datasets are single-task based and lack the ability to holistically evaluate multimodal LLMs much broad and general reasoning capability.
elements.sent-2411-042: For example, those typical datasets simply focus on single-task including image caption~\cite{lin2014microsoft , VQA~\cite{antol2015vqa,balanced_vqa_v2 and optical character recognition~(OCR)~\cite{liu2024hidden .}}}
elements.sent-2411-043: In the LLM era, more powerful and comprehensive multimodal datasets have been curated and released for various holistic and multi-task evaluation purposes, hugely scaling up the potential and possibility for designing various powerful multimodal large language model frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-044: Among all of them, MME~\cite{fu2023mme is one of the earliest benchmarks for multimodal Yes/No question, visual perception, and language reasoning tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-045: MMBench~\cite{liu2023mmbench extends to encompass much more diverse subjects and a more robust circular evaluation setting.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Seed-Bench~\cite{li2023seed,li2023seed2 introduces much more diverse input sources, such as multiple-image inputs and video.}
elements.sent-2411-047: MM-Vet~\cite{yu2023mmvet integrates six sub-features from previous datasets, resulting in multi-task reasoning capabilities like OCR, recognition-focused questions, and math.}
elements.sent-2411-048: Other than MME~\cite{fu2023mme , MMBench~\cite{liu2023mmbench , Seed-Bench~\cite{li2023seed,li2023seed2 and MM-Vet~\cite{yu2023mmvet that mostly focus on recognition, more recent datasets focus on more generalized artificial intelligence capability.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-049: For example, MMMU~\cite{yue2023mmmu has collected large-scale domain-knowledge-required questions to push the boundary of general artificial intelligence.}
elements.sent-2411-050: HallusionBench~\cite{guan2023hallusionbench datasets aims to the language hallucination and visual illusion phenomena.}
elements.sent-2411-051: MathVista~\cite{lu2023mathvista instead exclusively focuses on math-centered visual questions based on images such as charts, tables, and diagrams.}
elements.sent-2411-052: BLINK~\cite{fu2024blink datasets introduce human-level perception ability to achieve holistic visual perception.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Mega-Bench~\cite{chen2024mega-bench scales up the multimodal evaluation to over 500 real-world tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Although those various benchmarks, the corresponding benchmark focusing on spatial understanding in autonomous driving is missing and we tend to fill in the gap in this work.
elements.sent-2411-055: Recent advancements have seen MLLMs being directly employed for autonomous driving and embodied intelligence, which heavily depend on sophisticated spatial perception and reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-056: However, these works lack a detailed investigation into the spatial reasoning abilities of MLLMs to convince how reliable current MLLMs are on spatial information.
elements.sent-2411-057: In this section, we introduce how we construct the DriveMLLM Benchmark as below, where section~\ref{subsec:datasource provides detailed descriptions of the data source we build this benchmark.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Section~\ref{subsec:label_construction provides detailed label construction processes and filtering processes.}
elements.sent-2411-059: It collects rich sensor data, including images from six cameras covering a full $360^\circ$ field of view, along with LiDAR, radar, and GPS/IMU data.
elements.sent-2411-060: The dataset was captured in the urban environments of Boston and Singapore, featuring a diverse range of traffic conditions, weather scenarios, and times of day.
elements.sent-2411-061: This diversity ensures that the models are tested on a wide array of real-world driving situations, enhancing the robustness of the evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-062: For our benchmark, we focused on the images from the front-facing camera in the validation split of the nuScenes~\cite{nuScenes dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-063: This subset includes complex scenes with dynamic and static objects such as vehicles and pedestrians.
elements.sent-2411-064: The images are high-resolution and come with comprehensive annotations, making them ideal for tasks requiring detailed spatial understanding.
elements.sent-2411-065: This multi-step filtering ensured that each image supported unambiguous spatial reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-066: The data filtering process comprises four main steps detailed below.
elements.sent-2411-067: We began by extracting images from the front-facing camera in the validation set of the nuScenes~\cite{nuScenes dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-068: This initial selection yielded 6,019 images containing objects of interest, such as vehicles and pedestrians.
elements.sent-2411-069: To ensure suitability for spatial reasoning evaluation, we applied rigorous filtering criteria:.
elements.sent-2411-070: Edge-Heavy Filter: We excluded objects whose centers do not appear within the frame, as they are too marginal for effective evaluation (see Figure \ref{fig:filter (a)).}
elements.sent-2411-071: This preserves the integrity of the objects being analyzed.
elements.sent-2411-072: Object Size Filter: Pedestrians with bounding box widths smaller than 40 pixels and vehicles with bounding box areas less than 2,000 pixels were excluded, as they are too small for reliable assessment (see Figure \ref{fig:filter (b)).}
elements.sent-2411-073: This ensures adequate visibility and discernibility of the objects.
elements.sent-2411-074: Occlusion Filter: We removed images exhibiting significant occlusion, defined as overlapping objects where the intersection area exceeds 30\% of the smaller bounding box (see Figure \ref{fig:filter (c)).}
elements.sent-2411-075: This guarantees that each object remains sufficiently visible and not excessively obscured.
elements.sent-2411-076: Ambiguity Filter: To mitigate ambiguity, we excluded images containing multiple instances of the same object type (e.g., two pedestrians or several vehicles), as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:filter (d).}
elements.sent-2411-077: This ensures that each image presents a distinct, unambiguous object for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-078: After applying these filters, we retained 2,734 images with clear and visible objects.
elements.sent-2411-079: Despite the automated filtering, some images still exhibited issues such as ambiguities, occlusions, or unmarked objects (e.g., guard posts partially obstructing vehicles).
elements.sent-2411-080: We conducted a manual review to further refine the dataset, selecting 880 images that featured single, unambiguous, and clearly visible objects with discernible attributes.

elements.sent-2411-081: To address the absence of natural language descriptions in the nuScenes~\cite{nuScenes dataset—essential for evaluating multimodal LLMs—we generated standardized descriptive text for each object using a 13B-parameter InstructBLIP model to minimize bias toward specific LLM conventions.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Pedestrians were identified by clothing (e.g., ``the pedestrian wearing a red shirt''), and vehicles by color (e.g., ``the red truck''), ensuring consistency across descriptions and supporting robust evaluation of model spatial understanding.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=1.0\linewidth]{figures/answer_horizontal.pdf} \caption{Qualitative visualization on the generated answer to each spatial question.} \label{fig:answer}
elements.sent-2411-083: These tasks evaluate the model's ability to determine precise spatial information about objects within an image, requiring an understanding of pixel-level details.
elements.sent-2411-084: To provide an intuitive understanding, we visualize these tasks and present them in Figure~\ref{fig:intro .}
elements.sent-2411-085: Object Localization Coordinates: The model must identify and provide the exact coordinates \([x, y]\) of a specified object in the image.
elements.sent-2411-086: This task tests the ability to pinpoint objects accurately within the image plane.
elements.sent-2411-087: Question: Where is \{\} located in the image?.
elements.sent-2411-088: Object Bounding Box Determination: This task involves calculating the bounding box coordinates \([min\_x, min\_y, max\_x, max\_y]\) of a specific object.
elements.sent-2411-089: It assesses the model's capability to delineate the spatial extent of objects.
elements.sent-2411-090: Question: What is the bounding box of \{\} in the image?.
elements.sent-2411-091: Camera-to-Object Distance Estimation: The model computes the Euclidean distance from the camera to a specified object using inferred spatial information.
elements.sent-2411-092: This evaluates the model's depth perception and distance estimation skills.
elements.sent-2411-093: Question: How far is the camera from \{\}?.
elements.sent-2411-094: Vertical Distance Calculation: The task requires calculating the vertical distance between the camera and an object based on the object's \(z\)-coordinate.
elements.sent-2411-095: This tests the model's understanding of vertical spatial relationships.
elements.sent-2411-096: Question: What is the camera's vertical distance from \{\}?.
elements.sent-2411-097: These tasks assess the model's ability to understand and compare the spatial relationships between multiple objects within a scene.
elements.sent-2411-098: To further illustrate these concepts, we provide visual examples in Figure~\ref{fig:intro .}
elements.sent-2411-099: Leftmost/Rightmost Object Identification: The model determines which of two specified objects is positioned further to the left/right in the image, testing lateral spatial reasoning based on inferred \(x\)-coordinates.
elements.sent-2411-100: Question: Which is further left/right, \{\} or \{\}?.
elements.sent-2411-101: Front/Back Position Determination: The model assesses whether one object is in front of another or whether one object is behind another based on depth cues, evaluating understanding of \(z\)-coordinate relationships.
elements.sent-2411-103: Inter-Object Distance Measurement: This task involves calculating the Euclidean distance between two objects in three-dimensional space, assessing comprehensive spatial reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-104: Question: How far apart are \{\} and \{\}?.
elements.sent-2411-105: Horizontal Distance Calculation Between Objects: The model calculates the horizontal distance between two objects, defined as the absolute difference in their \(x\)-coordinates, testing precise lateral distance estimation.
elements.sent-2411-106: Question: What is the horizontal distance between \{\} and \{\}?.
elements.sent-2411-107: For each task, models are provided with an image and a corresponding question formatted as specified.
elements.sent-2411-108: To ensure consistency and facilitate automated evaluation, models must output their answers within designated markers.
elements.sent-2411-109: To evaluate the spatial reasoning capabilities of MLLMs within the autonomous driving context, we conducted experiments using the DriveMLLM benchmark described earlier.
elements.sent-2411-110: We selected a diverse set of state-of-the-art MLLMs for evaluation, including GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini, LLAVA one vision models (LLaVA-ov-7b and LLaVA-ov-72b), Qwen2-VL models (qwen2-vl-7b and qwen2-vl-72b), and Gemini-1.5-Flash.
elements.sent-2411-111: These models vary in size and training data, providing a comprehensive assessment across different architectures.
elements.sent-2411-112: We tested each model under three experimental settings: zero-shot, one-shot, and five-shot learning.
elements.sent-2411-113: In the zero-shot setting, models were evaluated without any task-specific examples.
elements.sent-2411-114: The one-shot and five-shot settings provided the models with one and five exemplars to assess their ability to leverage few-shot learning for improved performance.
elements.sent-2411-115: All models were prompted with the standardized questions and required to produce outputs adhering strictly to the specified format, ensuring fairness and consistency in evaluation.

elements.sent-2411-116: To quantitatively evaluate the performance of the models on the DriveMLLM benchmark, we define a set of metrics that measure the accuracy and efficiency of the models across various tasks.
elements.sent-2411-117: Let \( N \) denote the total number of samples in the test set.
elements.sent-2411-118: For each sample \( i \), the model produces a prediction, which is compared against the ground truth to compute an individual accuracy score \( acc_i \) and an efficiency indicator \( \text{eff _i \).}
elements.sent-2411-119: The overall metrics are then aggregated over all samples.
elements.sent-2411-120: The accuracy metrics quantify the correctness of the model's predictions for different types of tasks.
elements.sent-2411-121: The individual accuracy \( acc_i \) for each sample \( i \) is defined based on the task type as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-122: For binary classification tasks, such as Left/Right (L/R) judgment and Front/Behind (F/B) judgment, the individual accuracy \( acc_i \) is defined as: \begin{equation}acc_i = \begin{cases} 1, \text{if } p_i = y_i 0, \text{if } p_i \neq y_i \end{cases}\end{equation} where: - \( p_i \) is the model's predicted label for sample \( i \).
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}acc_i =  1, if  p_i = y_i 0, if  p_i \neq y_i \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-123: The overall accuracy \( acc \) is calculated as the mean of the individual accuracies:.
elements.paragraph-2411-009: Distance-Based Accuracy (Tasks RHD, RD, CVD, CD)
elements.sent-2411-124: For tasks involving distance measurements—Relative Horizontal Distance (RHD), Relative Distance (RD), Camera Vertical Distance (CVD), and Camera Distance (CD)—the individual accuracy \( acc_i \) is computed using: \begin{equation}acc_i = \frac{1}{1 + \alpha_d \|d_i - d_i^{\text{gt}}\|_1}\end{equation} where: - \( d_i \) is the model's predicted distance for sample \( i \).
elements.sent-2411-125: For the pixel position localization task, the individual accuracy \( acc_i \) is based on the Euclidean distance error between the predicted and ground truth coordinates: \begin{equation}acc_i = \frac{1}{1 + \alpha_p \| \mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_i^{\text{gt}} \|_2}\end{equation} where: - \( \mathbf{x _i = (x_i, y_i) \) are the model's predicted coordinates for sample \( i \).}
elements.sent-2411-126: For the bounding box localization task, accuracy is measured using the Intersection over Union (IoU) between the predicted and ground truth bounding boxes: \begin{equation}acc_i = \text{IoU}(B_i, B_i^{\text{gt}}) = \frac{|B_i \cap B_i^{\text{gt}}|}{|B_i \cup B_i^{\text{gt}}|}\end{equation} where: - \( B_i \) is the model's predicted bounding box for sample \( i \).
elements.sent-2411-127: The Aggregate Accuracy Score (\( \text{AccS \)) combines the individual accuracies across all tasks and samples: \begin{equation}acc = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N acc_i \quad \text{AccS} = \frac{1}{8} \sum_{j=1}^8 acc_j\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-128: Efficiency metrics assess the model's compliance with the required output format, which is crucial for automated evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-129: For each sample \( i \), we define the efficiency indicator \( \text{eff _i \) as: \begin{equation}\text{eff}_i = \begin{cases} 1, \text{correctly formatted} 0, \text{otherwise} \end{cases}\end{equation} The Efficiency Score (\( \text{EffS \)) is calculated as: \begin{equation}\text{eff} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^N \text{eff}_i\quad\text{EffS} = \frac{1}{8} \sum_{j=1}^8 \text{eff}_j\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-130: The models are assessed on both relative and absolute spatial reasoning tasks.
elements.sent-2411-131: In Table~\ref{tab:acc_result , we report the accuracy of each spatial evaluation as well as the aggregate accuracy score (AccS) for each model across different tasks.}

elements.sent-2411-172: Accurate spatial reasoning in outdoor environments—covering geometry, object pose, and inter-object relationships—is fundamental to downstream tasks such as mapping, motion forecasting, and high-level planning in autonomous driving.
elements.sent-2411-173: We introduce SURDS, a large-scale benchmark designed to systematically evaluate the spatial reasoning capabilities of vision language models (VLMs).
elements.sent-2411-174: Built on the nuScenes dataset, SURDS comprises 41,080 vision–question–answer training instances and 9,250 evaluation samples, spanning six spatial categories: orientation, depth estimation, pixel-level localization, pairwise distance, lateral ordering, and front–behind relations.
elements.sent-2411-175: We benchmark leading general-purpose VLMs, including GPT, Gemini, and Qwen, revealing persistent limitations in fine-grained spatial understanding.
elements.sent-2411-176: To address these deficiencies, we go beyond static evaluation and explore whether alignment techniques can improve spatial reasoning performance.
elements.sent-2411-177: Specifically, we propose a reinforcement learning–based alignment scheme leveraging spatially grounded reward signals—capturing both perception-level accuracy (location) and reasoning consistency (logic).
elements.sent-2411-178: We further incorporate final-answer correctness and output-format rewards to guide fine-grained policy adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-179: Our GRPO-aligned variant achieves overall score of 40.80 in SURDS benchmark.
elements.sent-2411-180: Notably, it outperforms proprietary systems such as GPT-4o (13.30) and Gemini-2.0-flash (35.71).
elements.sent-2411-181: To our best knowledge, this is the first study to demonstrate that reinforcement learning–based alignment can significantly and consistently enhance the spatial reasoning capabilities of VLMs in real-world driving contexts.
elements.sent-2411-182: We release the SURDS benchmark, evaluation toolkit, and GRPO alignment code through: \url{https://github.com/XiandaGuo/Drive-MLLM .}
elements.sent-2411-183: Understanding complex spatial structures—such as object orientation, relative position, and geometric layout—is from discrete images or sequential videos~\cite{barabas2017current serves as a fundamental challenge in embodied perception and multi-modal scene understanding.}

elements.sent-2411-186: In the vision community, spatial relation reasoning within single images has received growing attention~\cite{sparel_iceei , often leveraging datasets such as Visual Genome~\cite{visual_genome .}}

elements.section-2411-009: Post-Train Alignment for Boosting Spatial Understanding
elements.subsection-2411-010: Data Generation by Test-Time CoT Scaling
elements.sent-2411-240: RL training primarily enhances a VLM's performance based on existing knowledge~\cite{chu2025sft , while SFT functions serves as a process of knowledge injection.}
elements.sent-2411-241: We argue that models with smaller parameter sizes lack inherent reasoning capabilities and thus require SFT to introduce reasoning knowledge.
elements.sent-2411-242: Since the constructed dataset does not contain chain-of-thought (CoT) annotations, it is necessary to generate reasoning traces for each QA pair.
elements.sent-2411-243: To provide more insights for the community, we employ open-source models to generate CoTs.
elements.sent-2411-244: As shown at the bottom side of \autoref{fig:method , we first use the visual reasoning model QVQ to reflect on and reason through a sampled set of k QA pairs.}
elements.sent-2411-245: These thought processes are then summarized and distilled into reasoning rules consisting of generalizable solution steps using Qwen2.5-VL-72B.
elements.sent-2411-246: By feeding these rules alongside the original QA pairs into Qwen2.5-VL-72B, we generate new QA pairs annotated with CoTs.
elements.sent-2411-247: These outputs are subsequently validated and corrected by the model itself, resulting in an automated pipeline for constructing high-quality QA datasets with COT annotations.
elements.sent-2411-248: The prompts used to generate CoT reasoning for the data are provided in Appendix~\ref{supp:cot_generation .}
elements.sent-2411-249: This method is motivated by two empirical observations: 1) Directly generating CoTs in batch using QVQ is computationally expensive and often results in verbose, unstructured, or format-inconsistent outputs; 2) Relying solely on Qwen2.5-VL-72B for CoT generation leads to degraded output quality and increased hallucinations.
elements.sent-2411-250: After obtaining the CoT-augmented data, we use it to train the model to enhance its spatial reasoning capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-251: The training pipeline is illustrated at the top side of ~\autoref{fig:method .}
elements.sent-2411-252: We begin with SFT as the cold start of the full model, including the training of the visual encoder, multimodal projector, and language model, using the generated long CoT data.
elements.sent-2411-253: Then, we apply reinforcement learning using GRPO~\cite{shao2024deepseekmath to enhance the model’s capacity for spatial reasoning.}
elements.sent-2411-254: In each training instance, the model is prompted to sequentially generate three components: the bounding box of the queried object, a step-by-step reasoning trace, and the final answer.
elements.sent-2411-255: Given that spatial reasoning is inherently object-centric, we assign a localization reward of 1 if the predicted bounding box achieves an IoU greater than 0.5 with the ground-truth region.
elements.sent-2411-256: To encourage output fidelity, a format reward of 1 is given if the model adheres to the prescribed output structure.
elements.sent-2411-257: Additionally, an accuracy reward of 1 is granted when the final answer is correct.
elements.sent-2411-258: To promote logical coherence in reasoning, we introduce a logic reward inspired by Embodied-R~\cite{zhao2025embodied , which assesses whether the reasoning trace leads to a correct answer.}
elements.sent-2411-259: While the original approach uses a frozen reference model to evaluate consistency by feeding in both the reasoning trace and the question, we identify two limitations: (1) including the question can introduce answer leakage, as the trace often implicitly encodes the answer; and (2) relying on a static external model entails a trade-off between inference cost and reliability.
elements.sent-2411-260: To address both issues, we feed only the reasoning trace into the model under training, which serves as its own verifier.
elements.sent-2411-261: This design is efficient, incurs no additional computational overhead, and dynamically adapts to the model’s evolving capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-262: A scalar logic reward of 1 is assigned if the inferred answer from the reasoning trace matches the originally generated final answer; otherwise, the reward is 0.
elements.sent-2411-263: Implementation Details We assess a variety of models, including state-of-the-art open-source and proprietary models, as well as models specifically designed for spatial understanding.
elements.sent-2411-264: A random baseline is also included for comparison, and the evaluated models are summarized in Table~\ref{tab:comparison_other .}
elements.sent-2411-265: All models are prompted with standardized instructions and are required to generate outputs strictly adhering to a predefined format.
elements.sent-2411-266: We employ the sglang framework\footnote{\url{https://github.com/sgl-project/sglang to accelerate inference and reduce evaluation time.}}
elements.sent-2411-267: All training and evaluation are conducted on eight NVIDIA A800 GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-268: For supervised fine-tuning, models are trained for 2 epochs with a learning rate of $1 \times 10^{-6}$ and a warm-up ratio of 10\%.
elements.sent-2411-269: GRPO training is performed for 1 epoch using a maximum prompt length of 4096 tokens, an output length of up to 512 tokens, and generating 4 samples per prompt.
elements.sent-2411-270: The structured response format is provided in Appendix~\ref{supp:response_format .}

elements.sent-2411-271: Evaluation Metrics To quantitatively evaluate model performance on the SURDS benchmark, we define a set of evaluation metrics.
elements.sent-2411-272: For the Pixel Localization Estimation task, we adopt a centerness-based \cite{tian2019fcos metric.}
elements.sent-2411-273: For other tasks, a prediction receives a score of 1 if it matches the ground-truth answer, and 0 otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-274: Given $N$ QA pairs, the metric score for each task is computed as the average over all $N$ pairs.
elements.sent-2411-275: The final overall score is the average of all individual task scores.
elements.sent-2411-276: The evaluation results of different models are presented in Table \ref{tab:comparison_other .}
elements.sent-2411-277: Among proprietary models, Gemini performed the best, achieving top results across several multi-object metrics.
elements.sent-2411-278: Among open-source VLMs, Qwen also showed strong performance, ranking second in many metrics.
elements.sent-2411-279: The specialized spatial understanding model performed poorly across all tasks, while the SpatialBot model could not be evaluated on several metrics due to its lack of instruction-following capability.
elements.sent-2411-280: Our proposed method achieved first place in multiple single-object metrics, with a significant margin over the second-best model—for instance, a nearly 60\% improvement on the depth metric.
elements.sent-2411-281: It also achieves the highest overall score, outperforming the second-best approach by 14.25\%.
elements.sent-2411-282: We visualize example responses from different models across various tasks, as shown in Figure \ref{fig:answer .}
elements.sent-2411-283: It can be seen that our proposed model performs well across multiple tasks.
elements.sent-2411-284: Furthermore, we visualize the complete outputs of our proposed model across various tasks, demonstrating its strong reasoning capabilities, as illustrated in Appendix \ref{supp:reasoning_example .}

elements.subsection-2411-012: Discussion on Benchmarking Performance SOTA models
elements.sent-2411-285: Here, we discuss the quantitative results of our benchmark with respect to four key aspects: single-object evaluation, multi-object evaluation, the relationship between model size and performance, and the impact of fine-tuning.
elements.sent-2411-286: Our benchmark offers three converging lines of evidence that current open-source VLMs still lack robust spatial grounding.
elements.sent-2411-288: We conducted ablation studies on our proposed method.
elements.sent-2411-289: The first set of experiments focused on the composition of the reward.
elements.sent-2411-290: Since the basic GRPO framework inherently includes the format reward and accuracy reward, we do not ablate these two.
elements.sent-2411-291: Instead, we examine the effects of adding or removing the location reward and logic reward.
elements.sent-2411-292: As shown in Table \ref{tab:ablation , incorporating either the location or logic reward individually led to only marginal improvements in overall performance.}
elements.sent-2411-293: However, when both rewards were applied together, the model experienced a significant performance boost.
elements.sent-2411-294: These results suggest that without the supervision provided by the location reward, the model's object localization ability degrades—this ability forms the foundation of spatial reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-295: Building on this, the inclusion of logic supervision further enhances the model's consistency and spatial reasoning capability.
elements.sent-2411-296: The second set of experiments focuses on the value settings of the rewards.
elements.sent-2411-297: We compared the conventional reward setting of {0, 1 with an alternative setting of {-1, 1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-298: The results show that using {0, 1 yields better performance.}
elements.sent-2411-299: We attribute this to the sparsity of rewards during training—if the model receives a penalty every time it fails to reason correctly, it would accumulate mostly negative rewards.
elements.sent-2411-300: This causes gradients to remain negative or close to zero over extended periods, thereby hindering learning progress.

elements.sent-2411-301: Ablation of training We also conducted ablation studies on the training of the base model.
elements.sent-2411-302: Specifically, we compared the standard GRPO training setup (using only format reward and accuracy reward) with our proposed reward design that incorporates format reward, location reward, accuracy reward, and logic reward.
elements.sent-2411-303: Similarly, we applied the same training configurations to the SFT-pretrained model.
elements.sent-2411-304: The results are presented in Table \ref{tab:comparison_training .}
elements.sent-2411-305: Across both the non-SFT and SFT models, GRPO training improves overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-306: However, for the non-SFT model, adding the location and logic rewards did not lead to further improvements.
elements.sent-2411-307: In contrast, for the SFT model, incorporating location and logic rewards resulted in a significant performance boost.
elements.sent-2411-308: We attribute this difference to the weaker localization ability of the non-SFT model.
elements.sent-2411-309: Due to this limitation, the location reward remains sparse and provides limited training benefit, leading to negligible performance gains.
elements.sent-2411-310: We introduce SURDS, a large-scale benchmark comprising 41,080 VQA training instances and 9,250 evaluation samples that span six spatial reasoning categories—orientation, depth estimation, pixel-level localization, pairwise distance, lateral ordering, and front–behind relations.
elements.sent-2411-311: Benchmarking state-of-the-art general-purpose VLMs on SURDS exposes persistent shortcomings in fine-grained spatial understanding.
elements.sent-2411-312: To mitigate these deficiencies, we propose a reinforcement-learning framework that integrates spatially grounded reward signals with a reasoning-consistency objective.
elements.sent-2411-313: Extensive comparative and ablation experiments demonstrate that our approach yields substantial performance gains over existing VLMs while empirically validating the efficacy of both the training method and the reward design.
elements.sent-2411-314: Nonetheless, the method remains untested on larger-scale model variants, and the benefits of linear reward scaling and multi-stage GRPO schedules have yet to be clarified.. Acknowledgements.

elements.section-2411-013: Structured Response Format for the VQA Task
elements.sent-2411-326: Output their bounding boxes under the <location> tag.
elements.sent-2411-327: Next, reason carefully and step-by-step under the <think> tag to ensure logical accuracy and robustness, including any relevant error checks.
elements.sent-2411-328: Finally, provide a concise and definitive response in the <answer> tag.
elements.sent-2411-329: Use the following format: <location>[[Detected target object]: [xmin, ymin, xmax, ymax], ...]</location> <think>[Step-by-step reasoning with attention to detail and potential error checks]</think> <answer>[Final answer]</answer> \end{tcolorbox \begin{tcolorbox [colback=gray!5!white,colframe=gray!75!black, title=Structured Response Format without Location Tag, fonttitle= ] Reason carefully and step-by-step under the <think> tag to ensure logical accuracy and robustness, including any relevant error checks.}}
elements.sent-2411-330: Use the following format: <think>[Step-by-step reasoning with attention to detail and potential error checks]</think> <answer>[Final answer]</answer> \end{tcolorbox.}
elements.sent-2411-331: Task: \{task\} Answer: \{answer\} Please provide a detailed reasoning process, verify its accuracy, and then give your final answer clearly.
elements.sent-2411-332: Analyze these examples to identify the underlying, generalizable problem-solving principles.
elements.sent-2411-333: Examples: \{examples\} Present your findings as bullet points in this format: - Step 1: [core principle] - Step 2: [core principle] ...
elements.sent-2411-334: Ensure these rules can be applied broadly to similar questions.
elements.sent-2411-335: Does the reasoning in <think> logically support the conclusion in <answer>? 2.
elements.sent-2411-336: Are there any internal contradictions, logical errors, or missing steps in the reasoning? 3.
elements.sent-2411-337: Is the reasoning chain complete and valid? Provide your evaluation in the following format: <reason>[A concise justification of your assessment or a brief note confirming the reasoning's validity]</reason> <validation>Valid / Invalid</validation> Then, regardless of validity, output the full response in the following format: - Keep <answer> unchanged.
